Tafseer:
Word Analysis and Explanation of the Verses
(O Prophet, when you [divorce] women, divorce them for [the commencement of] their waiting period and keep count of the waiting period, and fear Allah, your Lord. Do not turn them out of their [husbands'] houses, nor should they leave [themselves] unless they commit a clear immorality. And those are the limits [set by] Allah. And whoever transgresses the limits of Allah has certainly wronged himself. You know not; perhaps Allah will bring about after that a [new] affair) (1).
(The significance of addressing the Prophet [peace and blessings of Allah be upon him]): As noted in the introduction, the address to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) here is not personal, but rather as the representative of the Ummah. The proof for this is that immediately after addressing him, the plural pronoun of address 'tallaqtum' (when you [all] divorce) is used, making it clear that these commands are for all Muslims. However, addressing the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) first greatly enhances the importance of these commands. The purpose is to create a sense of their greatness in the hearts of the people, so they realize that if the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) himself is bound by these limits, then others are even more so.
(Respecting the divine limits regarding divorce): In the pre-Islamic period of ignorance (Jahiliyyah), the common method of divorce was that whenever a husband grew angry with his wife for any reason, he would pronounce not just three but thousands of divorces in a single breath, without considering the consequences, and immediately throw her out of the house. He thought: 'Now that I have divorced her, why should I bear the cost of even a single meal for her in my house!' The severe harms of such a method of divorce for the woman, the man, the children, the entire family, and even the unborn child in the mother's womb, have been discussed in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah. Keeping these harms in view, the instruction is given here: do not forget the limits and conditions prescribed by Allah in the heat of anger and hatred. Those who do so do not harm God in the least, but rather wrong their own souls.
(when you [divorce] women, divorce them for their waiting period and keep count of the waiting period): It is stated that when anyone must divorce, they should divorce according to the waiting period (Iddah) and keep an accurate count of this period. This was explained in Surah Al-Baqarah, where it is stated: (Divorce is twice. Then, either keep [her] in an acceptable manner or release [her] with good treatment) (Al-Baqarah, 2:229). This shows that it is not permissible to separate from a wife by pronouncing three or more divorces in a single breath; rather, one should divorce over two months, during two periods of purity (tuhr), and then in the third period of purity, he may take her back if he wishes to retain her properly, or otherwise release her in accordance with established norms if the final decision is to sever ties. Counting this waiting period is necessary for both the husband and the wife. It is necessary for the wife because during this period she has the right to be taken back if the husband wishes to retain her, a right which terminates once the period expires. It is also necessary because if it becomes known during this period that the woman is pregnant, her waiting period will extend until childbirth, and during this entire time, the responsibility for her maintenance and housing lies on the man.
(and fear Allah, your Lord): It is stated: fear your Lord, fear Him; meaning, observe these prescribed limits despite the anger you harbor against your wife. If you break the limits, remember that you are breaking the limits of your Lord, whose obedience is obligatory upon you and from whose grasp and wrath no one can save you.
(Some wisdom behind the waiting period restriction in divorce): (Do not turn them out of their houses, nor should they leave unless they commit a clear immorality) - During this waiting period, neither do you have the right to expel them from their homes, nor is it permissible for them to leave on their own. Instead, both should reside together in the same house so that if there is any possibility of reconciliation and reform, this cohabitation may facilitate it. Allah Almighty dislikes the breaking of the marital bond. Divorce is a remedy of necessity; Allah has permitted it due to human exigencies, but it is the most disliked of all permissible things (Mubahat) in the sight of Allah. Thus, to save His servants from this, He has imposed the condition of a waiting period for divorce, and has made it mandatory for both husband and wife to live in the same house during this time, so that they can ponder coolly and decide whether there is any possibility of reconciliation and reform before taking the final step.
(During the waiting period, the husband's house is also the wife's house): In 'Do not turn them out of their houses', the word 'their houses' (buyutihinna) points to the reality that the man should not assume the house belongs solely to him. Just as it is his house, it is also the wife's house during the waiting period. Therefore, it is neither permissible for the man to expel the wife, nor is it permissible for the wife to leave in anger. Leaving here does not refer to going out for routine small needs, but to leaving the house with the intention of bidding it farewell.
(An exceptional circumstance): (unless they commit a clear immorality) - Only one scenario is excepted from this: if the husband divorced the wife due to an act that falls under 'clear immorality'. This obviously refers to adultery or its close precursors, as the word is not commonly used for lesser offenses. If a husband has witnessed such an act by the wife and divorced her in anger, then it is neither permissible to demand that he keep her in his house, nor can one expect any of the benefits for which the Shariah prescribed this cohabitation. A self-respecting man cannot be expected to find room in his heart for a woman whose betrayal has become known to him. For this reason, in cases of mutual imprecation (Li'an), the jurists consider it best to separate them, because even if the legal charge of adultery is neutralized by the wife's counter-oath, the counter-oath cannot change the husband's heart. Similarly, if someone divorced his wife on a basis related to clear immorality, it is futile to expect his heart to ever be cleared toward her.
(The harm of violating divine limits falls upon the violators themselves): (And those are the limits [set by] Allah. And whoever transgresses the limits of Allah has certainly wronged himself) - This is a highly important warning. It is stated: these are the limits established by Allah, so whoever dares to transgress them should remember that he will not harm Allah in the least, but will only wrong his own soul. The restrictions and obligations Allah has imposed on His servants are not for His own sake, but for the welfare of the servants themselves. Those who break them should remember that they are destroying their own personal, familial, and social interests.
(A great wisdom): (You know not; perhaps Allah will bring about after that a affair) - This points to the wisdom underlying the instruction 'Do not turn them out of their houses, nor should they leave'. It is stated: you do not know, perhaps Allah will bring about a situation that leads to reconciliation after the dispute. That is, during this period of cohabitation, both husband and wife may reflect on their attitudes, their estranged hearts may be reunited, and their breaking home may be restored. If this happens, it is highly pleasing to Allah. He loves to see hearts united and homes inhabited; He does not like discord that separates husband and wife, causing not only their separation but also separating children from their mother and mothers from their children.

(And when they have [nearly] fulfilled their term, either retain them according to acceptable terms or part with them according to acceptable terms. And bring to witness two just men from among you and establish the testimony for Allah. That is instructed to whoever should believe in Allah and the Last Day. And whoever fears Allah—He will make for him a way out. And He will provide for him from where he does not expect. And whoever relies upon Allah—then He is sufficient for him. Indeed, Allah will accomplish His purpose. Allah has already set for everything a [decreed] extent) (2-3).
(The man is not permitted to use his right to harm the woman): It is stated: when they are about to reach their term—meaning they enter the third period of purity—then make the final decision to retain them or part with them. If they are to be retained, do so in accordance with acceptable terms, just as a God-fearing Muslim keeps his wife. If the decision is to part, do so in accordance with the customary manner of honorable people, i.e., with kindness and giving them some resources. In Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 231, He says: (And do not keep them to hurt them, so that you transgress). That is, if you retain them, it should not be with the intention of keeping them captive under your control to torment them. Obviously, anyone can do so because he has the right to take her back, but if anyone uses this right to harm and torment the woman, he is using a right granted by God for a highly tyrannical purpose, the consequence of which is very severe.
(The importance of witnesses and testimony): (And bring to witness two just men from among you and establish the testimony for Allah) - Whether you decide to retain her as a wife or part with her, appoint two trustworthy Muslims from among yourselves as witnesses in both cases, so that no dispute can arise later. Otherwise, there is a risk of disputes over inheritance and other matters upon the death of either party. Jurists generally regard this instruction of witnessing as highly recommended (istihsan). In a good society, even if it remains recommended, it is sufficient; however, in these times, due to social decay, just as registration is adopted for marriage, if a similar registration process is adopted for divorce, it can prevent many disputes.
(and establish the testimony for Allah): This is an emphatic instruction to Muslims in general, and to witnesses in particular, to perform the duty of testimony for the sake of Allah. That is, first, one should not avoid giving testimony as far as possible; second, when it is time to testify, do so impartially and without fear, solely for the sake of Allah. Remember that Allah Almighty has honored the Muslims in this world with the position of 'witnesses of Allah on earth'; therefore, in any matter, a Muslim is not merely a witness for a person or party, but is a witness for Allah, and his reputation in the Ummah depends on this testimony.
(An essential requirement of faith in Allah and the Last Day): (That is instructed to whoever should believe in Allah and the Last Day) - It is stated that these three matters explained in the preceding verses are among the essential outcomes of faith in Allah and the Last Day. Those who claim to believe in Allah and the Hereafter are instructed to adhere to them; otherwise, their claim of faith will remain completely meaningless.
(A grand glad tiding for those who observe the divine limits): (And whoever fears Allah—He will make for him a way out) - This reassures those who observe the limits that they should rely on their Lord, for if they face any difficulty, Allah will make a way out for them. He tests His servants through difficulties; those who lose courage and turn away are abandoned by Him, but those who resolve to respect God's limits despite difficulties will eventually find their path made easy by Him: (And those who strive for Us—We will surely guide them to Our ways) (Al-Ankabut, 29:69).

(And He will provide for him from where he does not expect) - This further explains the preceding point: Allah Almighty will provide sustenance for him from sources he could never have imagined. We noted above that one of the major motives for pronouncing three divorces in a single breath and expelling the wife from the house, especially among the poor, is their economic situation. They think: 'Now that I have divorced this woman, why should I bear the burden of her maintenance for nothing!' Allah Almighty has reassured them: if you bear this burden to respect the limits of Allah, He will provide you with sustenance from where you do not expect. Do not think that the ways of God's mercy and help are limited only to what you have planned or can imagine; rather, His ways are countless, and they are known only when they open. At that time, a person is left amazed to see God's help appear from a direction he could never have conceived.
(And whoever relies upon Allah—then He is sufficient for him) - It is stated: the only condition for this is to place complete trust in God. If you trust Him, He is sufficient to assist you. He is not dependent on worldly causes and means; rather, all causes and means are subject to Him. The servant should not fear that God will be unable to change the unfavorable circumstances surrounding him. When He wills to help, nothing can stand in the way of His decree.
(A fixed time is set for the manifestation of Allah's help): (Indeed, Allah will accomplish His purpose. Allah has already set for everything a extent) - However, it is inevitable that just as Allah has appointed a time for everything, He has also appointed a time for the manifestation of His help. If there is some delay in its manifestation, it is meant to test the patience of His servants. The servant should remain assured that Allah will surely fulfill His promise. If there is a delay, it will only be to the extent necessary to test his patience, and this test is for his own benefit.
(A question and its answer): A question arises here: what is the ruling if someone does not follow the method of divorce explained in these verses, but pronounces three or more divorces at one time and separates from his wife? In response to this, there is a difference of opinion between the Hanafis and other Imams. According to other Imams, multiple divorces pronounced at one time by such a person are considered as only one divorce. However, according to the Hanafis, the divorces pronounced in this manner do take effect, though the person will be a sinner in the sight of Allah for failing to adopt the correct method.
In our view, both positions have minor shortcomings that need correction from the perspective of respecting the Shariah. If a person, despite such clear instructions in the Qur'an, pronounces multiple divorces in a single breath and faces no disciplinary action for this audacity, it means his mockery of the religion has been ignored. It is necessary to take notice of this; otherwise, the word 'divorce' becomes completely meaningless, whereas in the Shariah, the words of marriage and divorce are highly significant—even if spoken in jest, they take effect. From this perspective, the Hanafi position seems more aligned with respecting the Shariah, as they enforce the divorces; however, there is a flaw in this too. Specifically, if no disciplinary action or penalty is imposed, the mere fact that the person will be a sinner in the sight of Allah is not sufficient to cultivate proper respect for the Qur'anic method among the people. Therefore, while enforcing the divorce, he should also be penalized for playing with the religion, so that those who adopt this wrong method of divorce are discouraged.
The basis of this Hanafi fatwa lies in an ijtihad of Caliph Umar (may Allah be pleased with him). As far as we know, his ijtihad was that the divorces of such a person should be enforced because he has thrown away his own right of return (raj'ah), but at the same time, he should be punished for violating the limits of Allah so that others do not dare to violate the limits of the Shariah. This ijtihad is highly wise. We have set a specific limit for discussing detailed jurisprudential issues in this book, so we content ourselves with this brief reference; we have discussed this in detail in some of our jurisprudential essays.

(And those who no longer expect menstruation among your women—if you doubt, then their waiting period is three months, and [also for] those who have not menstruated. And for those who are pregnant, their term is until they give birth. And whoever fears Allah—He will make for him of his affair ease) (4).
(Difference in the waiting period of women due to their varying conditions): The waiting period was explained above for women who menstruate, whose period is determined by menstruation and purity. Now, the waiting period is explained for women who have reached menopause, those who have not yet menstruated, and those who are pregnant (preventing menstruation). It is stated: the waiting period for menopausal women and those who have not yet menstruated is three months, while the waiting period for pregnant women is until they give birth.
(A difficulty of commentators and its resolution): The condition 'if you doubt' (in irtabtum) attached to menopausal women has caused considerable confusion among commentators. Generally, people interpret it to mean: 'if you have a doubt regarding their waiting period, we inform you that it is three months.' Another opinion is: if a woman experiences bleeding but it is unclear whether it is menstruation or chronic bleeding (istihadah), her waiting period is three months.
The first opinion is narrated from Sa'id ibn Jubayr and was preferred by Ibn Jarir, but it does not seem strong. If the objective was merely to refer to the people's question, the most appropriate term for expressing this would be 'question', which is used everywhere in the Qur'an on such occasions; the word 'irtabtum' (you doubt) is not suitable for this. Secondly, if it were a question, it would apply to all three categories of women whose ruling is mentioned here, as is known from narrations. However, 'irtabtum' is used in a way that shows it is mentioned as a specific condition for menopausal women.
The second opinion is narrated from Mujahid, Zuhri, and Ibn Zayd. A drawback here is that if this issue concerned women who experience bleeding but face doubt as to whether it is menstruation or chronic bleeding, it would be inappropriate to describe them as 'those who no longer expect menstruation', whereas the text uses 'allai ya'isna min al-maheed' (those who no longer expect menstruation). Secondly, this would imply that a menopausal woman about whom there is no such doubt has no waiting period and can leave immediately after divorce. However, as far as we know, while there is no waiting period for an divorced woman with whom marriage was not consummated, a menopausal woman with whom marriage was consummated must observe a waiting period of three months.
Because of these doubts, my mind inclines to the view that the condition 'if you doubt' (in irtabtum) is used here to distinguish between a menopausal woman with whom marriage was consummated and one with whom it was not. That is, if the marriage was consummated, then despite her menopausal state, there remains a possibility that this cessation of menstruation is temporary, hope may arise again, and there may be something in her womb. The same situation can happen to a woman who has not yet menstruated but whose marriage has been consummated. On this basis, while there is no waiting period for a menopausal or young woman whose marriage was not consummated, those whose marriages were consummated must observe a waiting period because of the possibility of doubt. If someone asks: 'If this was the point, why was it not stated clearly that a menopausal woman whose marriage was consummated must observe a waiting period of three months?' The answer is: if it were stated that way, the actual cause (illah) of the waiting period would not be clear, whereas it was necessary to clarify it. The actual cause is not merely the consummation of marriage, but the suspicion that there might be a child in her womb. (And for those who are pregnant, their term is until they give birth) - It is stated: similarly, the waiting period for pregnant women is until they give birth.
(A question and its answer): A question arises regarding this ruling: in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 234, the waiting period of a widow is stated as four months and ten days, while here the waiting period of a pregnant woman is stated as childbirth. If a pregnant divorced woman's husband passes away, will she complete the four months and ten days or will her waiting period end immediately upon childbirth? The answer is: the waiting period of a pregnant woman cannot be determined by months and days. It is bound to childbirth in any case, which can be more or less than four months and ten days. If it is more, the woman is bound to complete it; so when it is less, she should also have the right to benefit from this reduction. Thus, these two rulings concern two different situations, and both remain applicable within their respective domains.
(Reassurance for those who observe the limits of the Shariah): (And whoever fears Allah—He will make for him of his affair ease) - The same point mentioned in verse 2 is repeated here with a slight variation in wording. These warnings alongside the commands are necessary so that people do not perceive the Shariah of Allah as a burden. The meaning is: maintaining the waiting period and the expenses for these divorced women will indeed seem heavy to some, but for those who fear Allah and establish His limits to the best of their ability, Allah Almighty will create ease. If His servants show the courage to bear the burdens He imposes on them, He assists them in bearing them and, as mentioned above, provides for them from where they do not expect. One should neither harbor the ill assumption that Allah will impose a burden beyond one's capacity, nor fear that He will abandon the servant after placing a burden on him.

(That is the command of Allah, which He has sent down to you; and whoever fears Allah—He will remove from him his misdeeds and make great for him his reward) (5).
This further emphasizes the preceding subject: these are the commands of Allah which He has revealed to you, so do not regard them as heavy, do not look down on them, and do not assume that He will remain unconcerned after revealing these commands to you. Rather, He will help you if you fulfill them, and He will punish you if you try to cast them aside. (and whoever fears Allah—He will remove from him his misdeeds and make great for him his reward) - This reassures them that those servants of Allah who try to establish His limits to the best of their ability will be treated with pardon and forgiveness by Him. He will forgive their minor errors and shortcomings, and multiply the reward of their good deeds.

(Lodge them [in a section] of where you dwell, out of your means, and do not harm them in order to oppress them. And if they should be pregnant, then spend on them until they give birth. Then if they breastfeed for you, give them their loss-compensation and consult among yourselves in an acceptable manner; but if you find yourselves in difficulty, then another woman will breastfeed for him) (6).
(The manner of lodging women during the waiting period): It is stated: the manner of lodging these women during the waiting period should not be such that their self-respect is injured. Rather, provide them with the same standard of lodging that matches your own standard of living based on your income, and do not adopt ways to torment them in any aspect so that they are forced to leave your house in distress after a few days. 'Wujd' means means or capacity. A person's standard of living is based on his income, and the instruction regarding these women is that they must be kept according to the same standard. It cannot be that the husband lives in villas and mansions while the wife is placed in a servants' quarter or garage, with leftover food sent to her.
(and do not harm them in order to oppress them): If someone has not accepted these commands of Allah from the heart, they may outwardly comply while adopting subtle ways to torment the woman so that spending even twenty-four hours in the house becomes impossible for her. If this happens, the entire wisdom for which these commands were given is lost. Therefore, the negative aspect is also clarified: do not make plans to torment them and drive them away. (And if they should be pregnant, then spend on them until they give birth) - This specifically instructs regarding pregnant women: spend on them until they give birth. The reason for mentioning them with special care is that their period of stay can be long, and under certain circumstances, their expenses can be of a different nature.
(Some rules regarding breastfeeding): (Then if they breastfeed for you, give them their loss-compensation and consult among yourselves in an acceptable manner) - This is the stage after childbirth: if they breastfeed your child, pay them their compensation, and settle this compensation through mutual consultation in an acceptable manner that matches the custom of the time and the man's standard of living.
(but if you find yourselves in difficulty, then another woman will breastfeed for him): That is, if both parties find it difficult to reach an agreement, then another woman may be employed to breastfeed the child. This means it depends on mutual consent, ease, and understanding between the parties. No one can be forced against their will in this matter. The basic rules of breastfeeding have been discussed under Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 233; refer to it for details, as this is not the place for explaining detailed jurisprudential issues.

(Let a man of wealth spend from his wealth, and he whose provision is restricted—let him spend from what Allah has given him. Allah does not charge a soul except [according to] what He has given it. Allah will bring about, after hardship, ease) (7).
(The standard of spending): This defines the standard of spending: the wealthy must spend according to their wealth, and the poor according to their income. It is neither permissible for the wealthy to keep them in a standard below theirs, nor will the poor be burdened beyond their capacity. Allah Almighty has placed responsibility on everyone only according to their capacity.
(Reassurance for the poor): (Allah will bring about, after hardship, ease) - This comforts the poor on the spot: if they remain content and patient in their state and make arrangements to establish God's limits despite their poverty, Allah Almighty will bring about ease for them after hardship. Allah blesses the sustenance of those who make sacrifices for His pleasure despite their poverty and need.

(And how many a city was insolent toward the command of its Lord and His messengers, so We took it to a severe account and punished it with a terrible punishment) (8).
(A general warning): To understand the context and position of this verse, refer to Surah At-Taghabun, verses 6-8. This is a general warning addressed to both the Quraysh and the Muslims: Allah has revealed His guidance for your direction. If you appreciate it, the benefit will reach you; but if you disobey, remember that many nations before you rejected the commands of their Lord and the teachings of His messengers, so Allah took them to a severe account and punished them with a terrible punishment. This refers to Aad, Thamud, and others whose accounts are narrated in the Qur'an, as well as the Jews, whom Allah blessed with His Shariah but they did not appreciate it, so they fell under His severe punishment.
In 'was insolent toward the command of its Lord' (atat an amri rabbiha), the preposition 'an' indicates that 'atat' implies turning away (a'radat); that is, out of rebelliousness, they turned away from the command of their Lord. 'We took it to account' (hasabnaha) refers to taking a severe account; meaning, Allah seized them severely and punished them terribly. 'Nukra' means severe and terrible.

(So it tasted the bad consequence of its affair, and the outcome of its affair was loss) (9).
That is, when they came under the grasp of God's accounting, they had to face the consequence of their rebelliousness, and their outcome was ruin. This is because the only path of success and welfare is the one shown by Allah and His Messenger. If nations deviate from it due to rebelliousness, they inevitably face ruin.

(Allah has prepared for them a severe punishment. So fear Allah, O you of understanding who have believed. Allah has indeed sent down to you a reminder) (10).
[This entry corresponds to the first part of the explanation of verses 10-11, as the Tafseer for these verses was combined in the original text.]

(A messenger reciting to you the distinct verses of Allah that He may bring out those who believe and do righteous deeds from darknesses into the light. And whoever believes in Allah and does righteousness—He will admit him into gardens beneath which rivers flow, to abide therein forever. Allah has indeed granted him a good provision) (10-11).
(A powerful warning and encouragement): In highly clear terms, this is both a warning and an effective encouragement for Muslims: Allah has revealed His reminder (the Qur'an) to you and sent His Messenger. Now, the next step is yours: do you appreciate the arrangement Allah has made to bring you out of darkness into light, or do you imitate those nations whose end was ruin and for whom Allah has prepared a severe punishment?
(So fear Allah, O you of understanding who have believed): That is, since Allah's way with the nations has been according to the established law mentioned above, O people of intellect who have believed, the path of wisdom is to fear your Allah. The point is: your taking steps in the path of faith proves that you are not slaves to desires but people who use their intellect; so now your faith requires you to fear your Allah and not deviate from His limits, lest your end be like that of the nations who deviated from the path shown by Allah.
(Intellect and faith are interconnected): The mention of 'who have believed' (alladhina amanu) after 'O you of understanding' (ya uli'l-albab) shows that there is an interconnected relationship between intellect and faith. A person of intellect must be blessed with faith. If someone is not blessed with faith, even if he is highly skilled in measuring the width and breadth of the earth and heavens, there is a major flaw in his intellect.
(Arrangement for guidance): (Allah has indeed sent down to you a reminder, a messenger reciting to you the distinct verses of Allah that He may bring out those who believe and do righteous deeds from darknesses into the light) - This points to the arrangement Allah made to establish the proof: He revealed His reminder to you and sent a Messenger who recites Allah's verses to those who choose the path of faith and righteous deeds, to bring them out of darkness into the highway of light. The point is: if you still prefer darkness over light after this arrangement, the responsibility will lie solely on you, and you will be unable to present any excuse before your Lord for this deviation.
(The life of the Messenger is a reminder of Allah): In 'a reminder, a messenger', the 'reminder' (dhikr) refers to the Holy Qur'an. The Qur'an is also described by the words 'dhikra' and 'dhi'l-dhikr'. We have pointed to the implications of this description in its relevant place: it serves as a reminder of all the innate qualities of human nature. Furthermore, it reminds of the guidance Allah sent for the direction of mankind which nations kept forgetting, reminds of how Allah dealt with those who rejected the messengers and those who believed in them, and above all, reminds of the recompense that is bound to manifest on a fixed Day after this life, which is the purpose and end of this world.
'A messenger' appears here as an exchange (badal) for 'a reminder', which proves that there is a relationship of spirit and form between the two. Just as the word 'reminder' (dhikr) is used for the Qur'an, the word 'reminder-giver' (mudhakkir) is used for the Messenger: (You are only a reminder-giver) (Al-Ghashiyah, 88:21). The life of the Messenger of Allah (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was entirely a reminder of the realities for which the Qur'an was revealed. That is, whatever the Qur'an taught, the Messenger demonstrated through his actions, establishing the proof of Allah upon the people so perfectly that no shortcoming remained in any aspect.
(Glad tiding of the Hereafter's provision): (Allah has indeed granted him a good provision) - That is, whoever chooses the path of faith and righteous deeds and makes sacrifices to protect the limits of Allah should remain assured that this is not a losing transaction; rather, Allah has prepared an excellent provision for them in the Hereafter. In verse 3 above, those who sacrifice and trust Him were given the glad tiding of divine support in this world.

(It is Allah who has created seven heavens and of the earth, the like of them. [His] command descends among them so that you may know that Allah is over all things competent and that Allah has encompassed all things in knowledge) (12).
(Reference to Allah's attributes to strengthen people's belief): Finally, Allah Almighty reminds of His attributes of power and knowledge, so that the belief in the matters mentioned above—whether related to warnings or comfort—becomes firmly established in the hearts of the people, and they know that Allah's power is limitless and His knowledge encompasses all things. Nothing in the seven heavens and seven earths is hidden from Him. If anyone does good, it is in His knowledge, and if anyone does evil, it is also in His knowledge. Obviously, unless this awareness regarding Allah is firmly established in the hearts, the limits of His Shariah cannot be properly respected.
(Seven heavens and seven earths): (It is Allah who has created seven heavens and of the earth, the like of them) - To give an idea of the vastness of His power, He says: do not assume that God's universe consists only of what you can see. This is a very small and limited part of His universe. This sky you see is only one; God has made seven heavens like it, and the earth is not just the one on which you walk, but there are also seven earths, one with each heaven.
The seven heavens are mentioned repeatedly in the Qur'an and other heavenly scriptures, but the seven earths are mentioned specifically in this Surah. However, when seven heavens are mentioned, the existence of seven earths with them becomes necessary. The relationship between heaven and earth is like that between a house and its ceiling; since the ceilings are seven, the houses must also be seven. Just as earth cannot be conceived without heaven, heaven cannot be conceived without earth. For height, the existence of depth is indispensable.
As for the question of whether the same laws and systems operate in these seven heavens and earths, and whether the same kind of creation inhabits them or different creations and systems exist, the mere word 'the like of them' (mithlahunna) does not require that the same creation and systems exist in every world. This likeness relates only to creation—that just as Allah made seven heavens, He also made seven earths. As for their systems and laws, Allah has not informed us of them, nor are they within our comprehension. For us, this general belief is sufficient:
'There are other worlds beyond the stars.'
Our science is still wandering in the infinite space. It reveals one secret, and hundreds of other mysteries arise from it. However, there is no reason to despair. If science cannot find the clue, spiritual courage will eventually open all secrets. The knowledge given to human beings in this world is very small: (And you have not been given of knowledge except a little) (Al-Isra, 17:85).
(His command descends among them): That is, just as God's commands and laws descend between your heaven and earth, His commands also descend within the other heavens and earths. (so that you may know that Allah is over all things competent) - That is, Allah has revealed this secret of His universe to you so that the reality becomes entirely clear to you that there are no limits to Allah's power. Before 'la-ta'lamu' (so that you may know), an appropriate verb is omitted, and examples of such omission have appeared previously.
(and that Allah has encompassed all things in knowledge): This explains the second purpose: just as there are no limits to His power, there are also no limits to His knowledge. His knowledge encompasses everything in all these worlds. With the grace and favor of Allah, the commentary of this Surah is completed. All praise and favor belong to Allah. Rahmanabad, April 25, 1978 / Jumada al-Awwal 16, 1398 AH.
